g.data-narration: Welcome to Module 5: We explore how processors work, from datapaths to the fetch-decode-execute cycle.
g.text: Fundamental Concepts of Digital Design & Computer Organization

g.data-narration: We transition from building components like registers and ALUs to understanding how they form a programmable computer system.
g.text: We move from hardware design (combinational/sequential logic) to
understanding how components integrate into a functional CPU.

g.data-narration: The processor executes instructions through the datapath and control unit, running the fetch-decode-execute cycle billions of times per second.
g.text: The CPU is the heart of computation, consisting of:

g.data-narration: The datapath is the highway of the processor—a network of functional units like ALU and registers connected by buses for data flow.
g.text: The datapath is the "highway" where actual computations happen. It contains:
• Registers for holding operands and results

g.data-narration: Key components include: Program Counter (holds next instruction address), Register File (fast internal memory), and ALU (computational heart).
The Control Unit is the brain that examines instructions and generates control signals to operate multiplexers, registers, and the ALU.
g.text: The Control Unit examines the instruction opcode and generates control signals
to direct data flow through the datapath—like a conductor orchestrating an orchestra.

g.data-narration: For ADD R1,R2,R3, the control unit must read R2 and R3, select ALU addition, and write result to R1.
g.text: Instruction: ADD R1, R2, R3 (R1 = R2 + R3)

g.data-narration: The Fetch-Decode-Execute cycle is the fundamental process the CPU repeats billions of times per second to process instructions.
g.text: This is the fundamental process the CPU repeats billions of times per second.
It has five key stages that form the core of computer operation:

g.data-narration: In Instruction Fetch: PC provides address, memory returns instruction into IR, and PC increments to next instruction.
In Decode: Control unit interprets the instruction opcode to determine the operation and reads operands from the register file.
g.text: Example: Is this ADD? LOAD? SUB?

g.data-narration: In Execute: The ALU performs the operation on operands—for memory instructions, the effective address is calculated here.
In Memory Access: If the instruction needs memory (read/write), this is when the actual memory operation occurs.
In Write-Back: The result from ALU or memory is written to the destination register in the register file.
Hardwired control uses FSM circuits (fast but rigid); Microprogrammed uses control store memory (flexible but slower).
Example: LOAD R1, [1000] fetches from PC, decodes LOAD, calculates address, reads memory, writes to R1.
Key takeaways: Datapath is the body, control unit is the brain, fetch-decode-execute is the heartbeat of computation.

g.text: ✓ Datapath: The "body" - where data is processed
✓ Control Signals: Make hardware work in concert

g.data-narration: These fundamentals apply to all modern processors—CPUs, GPUs, embedded controllers—forming the essential link between hardware and software.
g.text: These concepts are fundamental to designing:
• Modern Microprocessors (Intel, AMD, ARM) • GPUs • Embedded System Controllers
Essential for: Hardware Engineering, FPGA Programming, Hardware Security
